event: Upper Paleolithic peoples create the rock engravings of the Côa Valley, one of Europe's largest open-air collections of prehistoric art.
Neolithic communities along the Atlantic coast build megalithic monuments such as the dolmens and the great cromlech of the Almendres.
Phoenician and later Carthaginian traders establish coastal contacts and settlements in the south of the Iberian Peninsula.
Celtic peoples migrate into the region, blending with native populations to form the Lusitanian and Gallaecian cultures.
Rome begins its conquest of the Iberian Peninsula during the Second Punic War against Carthage.
The Lusitanian leader Viriathus, who long resisted Roman expansion, is assassinated, breaking native resistance.
Rome organizes the region within the province of Lusitania, founding cities such as Olisipo (Lisbon) and Emerita Augusta.
Germanic Suebi and other tribes cross into Iberia, and the Suebi establish a kingdom in the northwest centered on Bracara.
The Visigothic king Leovigild conquers the Suebi kingdom, bringing the region under Visigothic rule.
Muslim Umayyad forces invade Iberia, rapidly conquering most of the peninsula including the future Portuguese lands.
Vímara Peres reconquers Porto and establishes the County of Portugal under the Kingdom of Asturias-León.
Henry of Burgundy is granted the County of Portugal by King Alfonso VI of León and Castile.
Afonso Henriques defeats the Moors at the Battle of Ourique and assumes the title of King of Portugal.
The Treaty of Zamora recognizes Portugal's independence, with Afonso I as its first king.
Afonso I captures Lisbon from the Moors with the aid of crusaders, a key step in the Reconquista.
Pope Alexander III formally recognizes Portugal as an independent kingdom in the bull Manifestis Probatum.
King Afonso III completes the Reconquista in Portugal with the capture of the Algarve from the Moors.
King Dinis founds the University of Coimbra, one of the oldest universities in Europe.
Portugal and England sign the Anglo-Portuguese Alliance, the oldest active diplomatic alliance in the world.
John I defeats Castile at the Battle of Aljubarrota, securing independence and founding the House of Aviz.
Portugal captures Ceuta in North Africa, marking the start of its overseas expansion and the Age of Discovery.
Portuguese navigators sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator discover and begin settling the Madeira archipelago.
Bartolomeu Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope, opening the sea route around Africa toward the Indian Ocean.
The Treaty of Tordesillas divides newly discovered lands between Portugal and Spain along a meridian.
Vasco da Gama reaches India by sea, establishing a direct maritime trade route between Europe and Asia.
Pedro Álvares Cabral reaches the coast of Brazil, claiming it for Portugal.
Luís de Camões publishes the epic poem Os Lusíadas, the defining work of Portuguese literature.
Philip II of Spain seizes the Portuguese crown, beginning the Iberian Union of the two kingdoms.
Portugal revolts against Spanish rule, restoring independence under King John IV of the House of Braganza.
A catastrophic earthquake, tsunami, and fire devastate Lisbon, prompting the rebuilding led by the Marquis of Pombal.
Napoleon's forces invade Portugal, and the royal family flees to Brazil, transferring the court to Rio de Janeiro.
The Liberal Revolution of Porto demands a constitutional monarchy and the king's return from Brazil.
Brazil declares independence from Portugal, ending the kingdom's largest colonial possession.
The Liberal Wars erupt between supporters of absolutist Miguel I and the constitutionalists backing Pedro and Maria II.
A republican revolution overthrows the monarchy and establishes the First Portuguese Republic.
Portugal enters World War I on the side of the Allies after Germany declares war.
A military coup ends the unstable First Republic and ushers in an authoritarian dictatorship.
António de Oliveira Salazar establishes the corporatist authoritarian regime known as the Estado Novo.
Portugal becomes a founding member of the NATO military alliance.
Portugal is admitted to the United Nations.
The Portuguese Colonial War begins as independence movements rise in Angola, then Guinea-Bissau and Mozambique.
The Carnation Revolution overthrows the Estado Novo dictatorship in a largely peaceful military coup.
Portugal grants independence to its African colonies, including Angola, Mozambique, and Guinea-Bissau.
Portugal adopts a new democratic constitution and holds free elections under the Third Republic.
Portugal joins the European Economic Community, anchoring its democracy and economy within Europe.
Lisbon hosts the Expo '98 world exposition and writer José Saramago wins the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Portugal adopts the euro as its currency and transfers Macau to China, ending its last overseas territory.
Euro banknotes and coins enter circulation, replacing the Portuguese escudo.
Amid the eurozone debt crisis, Portugal accepts a major international bailout and adopts austerity measures.
Portugal wins its first major football title, claiming victory at the UEFA European Championship.
